results.Name: ISSA 5000 General Requirements for Sustainability Assurance Engagements - Final Pronouncement November 2024
ISSA 5000 General Requirements for Sustainability Assurance Engagements - Basis for Conclusions Prepared by the Staff of the IAASB
Achieving Consistent and Comparable Climate-related Disclosures: 2024 Progress
results.notes:
The Financial Stability Board (FSB) published [url=https://www.fsb.org/uploads/P121124.pdf][i]Achieving Consistent and Comparable Climate-related Disclosures: 2024 Progress[/i][/url], which describes the progress FSB jurisdictions, standard-setters, and international organizations have made in achieving consistent and comparable climate-related disclosures. The FSB, which includes the U.S. Federal Reserve, Treasury, and Securities and Exchange Commission, along with representatives from G20 economies, was formed in 2009 to coordinate national financial authorities and international standard-setting bodies in developing strong regulatory, supervisory, and other financial sector policies.

The report finds that around 75% of FSB jurisdictions have implemented or proposed voluntary or mandatory climate-related disclosure requirements based on the Task Force on Climate-related Financial Disclosures (TCFD) recommendations; NAIC climate disclosure standards follow TCFD guidelines. The International Sustainability Standards Board (ISSB), the standard-setting body established by the International Financial Reporting Standards (IFRS) Foundation, established the S1 and S2 sustainability disclosure standards in 2023; S2 formalizes TCFD principles. The report finds that approximately 57% of global GDP is represented by jurisdictions that have progressed toward adopting or aligning with the ISSB’s IFRS S1 and S2 standards. The report finds that adoption faces challenges, particularly in emerging markets and developing economies (EMDEs), due to regulatory uncertainty, limited data availability, perceived costs, and capacity gaps.

The FSB emphasizes the critical integration of sustainability-related disclosures with financial and prudential reporting, with initiatives such as the Basel Committee’s Pillar 3 framework helping to harmonize reporting requirements. Enhancing the reliability of disclosures is a key focus, with efforts to establish robust global assurance frameworks. This aligns with the IAASB ISSA 5000, which supports consistent assurance practices across jurisdictions.

results.Name: "Fit-For-55” Climate Scenario Analysis
results.notes: [b]The European Supervisory Authorities (EBA, EIOPA, and ESMA – the ESAs), together with the European Central Bank (ECB), [/b][color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://www.eiopa.europa.eu/document/download/6a90e0a7-135e-41f0-af69-22065a0bf8cd_en?filename=Report%20on%20fit%20for%2055%20climate%20scenario%20analysis.pdf][b][i]"Fit-For-55” climate scenario analysis[/i][/b][/url][/color][b][i], [/i]finds that climate transition risks (i.e., adapting to and mitigating climate-related risks) alone are unlikely to threaten financial stability. [/b]The European Union’s [i]Fit-for-55[/i] package aims to stimulate investment and innovation in the transition to a green economy, playing a key role in the E.U.’s goal to achieve an emissions reduction of 55% by 2030 and climate neutrality by 2050. It aims to align E.U. legislation with these goals through policies that include—among others—the E.U. emissions trading system, the carbon border adjustment mechanism, sector-specific emissions targets, and revisions to the renewable energy and energy efficiency directive. The European Commission [color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://www.eba.europa.eu/sites/default/files/document_library/About%20Us/Missions%20and%20tasks/Correspondence%20with%20EU%20institutions/2023/Assessment%20of%20the%20financial%20system%E2%80%99s%20resilience%20to%20stress%20in%20the%20transition%20to%20the%20EU%E2%80%99s%202030%20goals%20for%20the%20reduction%20of%20greenhouse%20gas%20emissions/Man/1052659/Mandate%20for%20the%20FF55%20one-off%20exercise.pdf]invited[/url][/color] the ESAs and the ECB to assess the impact on the insurance and other financial sectors and the potential for contagion and amplification effects across the financial system. The study did find that the interaction of adverse macro-financial developments with transition risk factors could disrupt the evolving transition and substantially increase financial institutions’ losses, thereby impairing their financing capacity.

results.Name: Instructions and Guidance on the Application of the Prudent Person Principle
results.notes:
In response to structural shifts in insurers' business models, including increased allocation to complex, illiquid, and hard-to-value assets, the Bermuda Monetary Authority (BMA) has increased its supervisory oversight. In this latest round, the BMA proposes [color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://cdn.bma.bm/documents/2024-12-04-13-09-32-Consultation-Paper---Proposed-Instructions-and-Guidanceon-the-Application-of-the-Prudent-Person-Principle.pdf]Instructions and Guidance on the Application of the Prudent Person Principle[/url][/color] (PPP), building on the existing PPP implementation requirements. The PPP requires that an insurer only assume investment risks that it can properly identify, measure, respond to, monitor, control, and report on while considering its capital requirements and adequacy, short-term and long-term liquidity requirements, and policyholder obligations. Key points of the proposal include:
[ml][ul][li indent=0 align=left][b]Investment strategy.[/b] Insurers must adopt investment strategies prioritizing capital preservation and returns while aligning with policyholder obligations and liquidity needs.[/li][li indent=0 align=left][b]Investment risk management.[/b] A robust framework for managing investment risks, including stress testing and monitoring concentrations, liquidity mismatches, and derivatives usage.[/li][li indent=0 align=left][b]Governance.[/b] Boards and senior management are responsible for overseeing compliance with PPP, with a particular focus on governance structures, conflicts of interest, and risk monitoring.[/li][li indent=0 align=left][b]Outsourcing of investment-related services.[/b] When investment management is outsourced, insurers must ensure adherence to the PPP by external asset managers and retain adequate in-house expertise.[/li][li indent=0 align=left][b]Asset-Liability Matching.[/b] Ensuring investments align with liabilities’ nature, liquidity, and duration.[/li][li indent=0 align=left][b]Risk concentration, risk accumulation, and diversification. [/b]An insurer’s investment policy and limits should be such that individual investments and exposures to different asset classes and risk factors are held and maintained at prudent levels.[/li][li indent=0 align=left][b]Complex and non-publicly traded assets[/b]. In considering investing in complex investments, insurers should comprehensively assess the inherent risks that may be present, including the heightened risk of large, sudden, or unexpected losses due to limited transparency and information flow, volatility misestimation, and difficulties in identifying and reflecting changes in risk profile before significant deterioration.[/li][li indent=0 align=left][b]Solvency self-assessment and group solvency self-assessment. [/b]Insurers’ own risk and solvency self-assessment should be evidence and attest to their application of PPP and the considered impact on their solvency and liquidity positions.[/li][li indent=0 align=left][b]Use of derivatives and other financial instruments. [/b]An insurer choosing to engage in derivative activities should clearly define its objectives, investment and derivative strategy, governance mechanism, and derivative use policies and processes. Derivatives should be considered in the context of a prudent overall asset-liability management strategy.[/li][/ul][/ml]

results.Name: Insurance Group Supervision Framework
results.notes:
The Bermuda Monetary Authority (BMA) is proposing significant enhancements to its [color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=http://file///C:/Users/amnon/Downloads/2024-12-04-16-17-14-Consultation-Paper---Proposed-Enhancements-to-the-Insurance-Group-Supervision-Framework-Package-Current.pdf][i]Insurance Group Supervision Framework[/i][/url][/color] to align more closely with global standards, including the International Association of Insurance Supervisors (IAIS) Common Framework (ComFrame) and Insurance Core Principles (ICPs). Key aspects of the consultation paper (i.e., request for comment) include:
[ml][ul][li indent=0 align=left][b]Mandatory Group Supervision. [/b]The BMA will enforce mandatory group supervision if the ultimate parent entity of an insurance group is incorporated in Bermuda and oversees insurance operations both locally and abroad. This moves towards a direct supervision model, focusing on the parent entity.[/li][li indent=0 align=left][b]Definition and Supervision of Insurance Holding Companies. [/b]The proposal defines an [i]insurance holding company[/i] as any Bermuda-incorporated entity holding stakes in insurers. The BMA will directly regulate these entities when deemed critical to the group's financial health.[/li][li indent=0 align=left][b]Transition from Designated Insurers. [/b]The current framework uses a hybrid model, where group supervision often operates through a [i]designated insurer.[/i] The proposal suggests phasing out this approach, giving the BMA direct powers over parent or holding companies​.[/li][li indent=0 align=left][b]Direct Regulatory Powers. [/b]The proposal includes a robust set of powers for direct oversight, ensuring compliance, and addressing risks associated with insurance groups and their holding companies​.[/li][/ul][/ml]

results.Name: Liquidity Preparedness for Margin and Collateral Calls
results.notes:
The Financial Stability Board (FBC) published its final report, [url=https://www.fsb.org/uploads/P101224-1.pdf][i]Liquidity Preparedness for Margin and Collateral Calls[/i][/url]; the FSB had requested comment on its previous April 17, 2024, report. The report highlights the need for policy adjustments to deal with liquidity strains with non-bank financial intermediation (NBFI) arising from spikes in margin and collateral calls during market stress, such as the March 2020 market turmoil. NBFI includes life insurers who rely heavily on long-dated interest rate derivatives, which can be susceptible to margin calls. The FSB proposes eight high-level and cross-sectoral policy recommendations, noting that the regulatory landscape for NBFIs varies significantly. For example, while Solvency II in the E.U. and the U.K. has general requirements for insurance firms to assess and manage liquidity risk, there are no specific margin and collateral call rules. The FSB, which includes the U.S. Federal Reserve, Treasury, Securities and Exchange Commission, and representatives from G20 economies, was formed in 2009 to coordinate national financial authorities and international standard-setting bodies to develop strong regulatory, supervisory, and other financial sector policies. The FSB has been flagging concerns over liquidity risks, and in October 2024, the FSB published [url=https://www.fsb.org/uploads/P231024.pdf][i]Depositor Behaviour and Interest Rate and Liquidity Risks in the Financial System Lessons from the March 2023 Banking Turmoil[/i][/url], which points to margin and collateral calls on derivative positions or securities financing transactions as a main channel whereby insurers are most vulnerable to solvency and liquidity risks. 

The report provides eight policy recommendations intended to build on existing rules for governing non-bank market participants, including insurers, in managing margin and collateral calls in centrally and non-centrally cleared derivatives and securities markets, including securities financing such as repo (paraphrased):
[ml][ol][li indent=0 align=left]Liquidity risk arising from exposure to margin and collateral call spikes should be included in liquidity risk management and governance frameworks.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]Establish liquidity risk tolerances for margin and collateral calls and contingency funding plans to meet liquidity needs.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]Liquidity risk frameworks need to be reviewed regularly to ensure their ongoing effectiveness in mitigating liquidity risk exposures to spikes in margin and collateral calls, including during times of stress.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]Conduct liquidity stress tests to identify strain sources and ensure adequate funding and collateral.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]There is a need for liquidity stress tests to cover a range of extreme but plausible scenarios, including both backward-looking and hypothetical scenarios.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]There is a need for resilient and effective operational processes and collateral management practices.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]There is a need for sufficient cash, readily available and diverse liquid assets, and collateral arrangements to meet margin and collateral calls.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]There is a need for active, transparent, and regular interactions with counterparties and third-party service providers in collateralized transactions.[/li][/ol][/ml][b]Why does this matter? [/b]While the FSB is not a regulator, it shapes international policy by promoting financial stability and encouraging coherent policies across jurisdictions. For life insurers, the recommendations are particularly relevant given their potential exposure to liquidity risks (e.g., coming from surrenders or margin calls) in stressed markets. Regulatory bodies like the International Association of Insurance Supervisors (IAIS) will likely consider these insights, signaling heightened expectations for liquidity risk management and disclosure in the insurance sector. Insurers should anticipate evolving regulations and adjust their strategies accordingly.

results.Name: U.S. Insurers’ Collateralized Loan Obligation (CLO) Exposure Continues to Climb in 2023 but at a Slower Pace
results.notes:
The NAIC’s Capital Markets Bureau published its latest report, [color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://content.naic.org/sites/default/files/capital-markets-special-reports-clo-ye2023-final.pdf][i]U.S. Insurers’ Collateralized Loan Obligation (CLO) Exposure Continues to Climb in 2023 but at a Slower Pace[/i][/url][/color]. The report summarizes familiar 2023 CLO investment trends, which we reported details on in our [i]In the Weeds[/i] columns in [color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://mailchi.mp/bridgewayanalytics/newsreel-16531344?e=9ea3ec665c]ART Newsreel | May 2, 2024[/url][/color], for life companies, and [color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://mailchi.mp/bridgewayanalytics/newsreel-16532080?e=9ea3ec665c]ART Newsreel | May 9, 2024[/url][/color], for property & casualty. A few highlights from the report: 
[ml][ul][li indent=0 align=left]U.S. insurers’ CLO holdings were $271.2 (3.2% of total cash and invested assets) at year-end 2023, a 9.5% increase from $247.7 billion at year-end 2022. Life holdings increased, reaching $217.1 billion in 2023 from $192.3 billion in 2022, while property & casualty decreased to $46.3 billion in 2023 from $47.4 in 2022.  [/li][li indent=0 align=left]The pace of growth in insurers’ CLO investments was slower in 2023, down from almost 15% in 2022 and the 20% range between 2019 and 2021.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]The credit quality of U.S. insurers’ CLO investments remained predominantly investment grade, with tranches rated BBB and higher accounting for 82% of total CLO investments and those rated AAA increasing one percentage point to 41%.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]U.S. insurers’ exposure to commercial real estate (CRE) CLOs has also increased over the last few years (Chart 5 reproduced). The Capital Markets Bureau, [color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://content.naic.org/sites/default/files/capital-markets-primer-commercial-real-estate-loan-obligations.pdf][i]CRE CLO Primer[/i][/url][/color] provides useful context to this asset class and how it differs from traditional Commercial Mortgage Backed Securities. The typical investment includes short-term floating CRE loans, usually three to five years, departing from traditional fixed-rate and long-term investments under a CMBS structure. It has been argued that shorter-duration investments allow the CLO structure to manage its longer-duration liabilities better through market fluctuations. U.S. insurers held approximately $17 billion in CRE CLOs at year-end 2023, representing a 27% increase from $13.4 billion at year-end 2022.[/li][/ul][/ml][center][img width=500]https://mcusercontent.com/79c3cb5ae113df9ea96a5e443/images/19f909ca-1e9d-c15d-52db-273218ce5464.png[/img][/center]

[color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]The European Insurance and Occupational Pension Authority (EIOPA) published its [/color][color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://www.eiopa.europa.eu/document/download/024725cf-a9e7-4727-9f14-2a4fed6c6d66_en?filename=EIOPA%20Financial%20Stability%20Report%20December%202024.pdf]December 2024 Financial Stability Report[/url][/color][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)], which includes an analysis of offshore asset-intensive reinsurance. The report points to the increasing use of cross-border asset-intensive reinsurance (or funded reinsurance), which is facing increased regulatory scrutiny in several jurisdictions. It points to the June 2023 U.K. [/color][color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://www.bankofengland.co.uk/-/media/boe/files/prudential-regulation/letter/2023/june/thematic-review-work-on-funded-reinsurance-arrangements.pdf]Prudential Regulation Authority (PRA) warnings over the systemic use of funded reinsurance[/url][/color][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)] and the [/color][color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://www.iaisweb.org/2023/07/iais-publishes-preview-of-2023-global-monitoring-exercise-results/]International Association of Insurance Supervisors (IAIS) examination of supervisory cross-border asset-intensive reinsurance[/url][/color][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)].[/color]
[color=rgb(34, 34, 34)] [/color]
[b][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]EIOPA finds that of Europe's EUR 6,340 billion 2023 total life liabilities, only about 2.5% is reinsured, and less than 1% is reinsured offshore.[/color][/b][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)] The report concludes that reinsurance cannot be considered a source of systemic risk for the European life insurance sector.[/color]

[b][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]What's next?[/color][/b][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)] EIOPA plans to continue monitoring developments regarding the use of offshore asset-intensive reinsurance and will work toward improved Solvency II reporting on reinsurance.[/color]

results.Name: Evolution of Asset Intensive Insurance Report
results.notes:
[color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://cdn.bma.bm/documents/2024-12-13-16-43-44-Evolution-of-Asset-Intensive-Insurance-Report-White-Paper.pdf][i]Evolution of Asset Intensive Insurance Report[/i][/url][/color], is an update to the BMA December 18, 2023, report [color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://cdn.bma.bm/documents/2024-02-12-11-00-50-Supervision-and-Regulation-of-Private-Equity-Insurers.pdf][i]Supervision and Regulation of Private Equity (PE) Insurers in Bermuda[/i][/url][/color][i]. [/i]The paper aims to enhance the understanding of the potential risks of asset-intensive insurance businesses, including strategic alliances between life and annuity insurers and asset management firms and their governance. It argues that strategic alliances between life and annuity insurers and asset management firms have played a significant role in narrowing the estimated $51 trillion global protection gap, with private capital and tools such as reinsurance playing a critical role. However, these partnerships have led to a notable shift in traditional investment strategies and pose unique risks and supervisory challenges, primarily arising from two main risk drivers:
[ml][ul][li indent=0 align=left]Potential governance, conflict, and risk management challenges; and[/li][li indent=0 align=left]A tendency to invest in higher proportions of illiquid assets, which are subject to valuation, concentration, and complexity risks (and may be subject to elevated spread risk, particularly in stressed conditions).[/li][/ul][/ml]The report identifies important guardrails in the (re)insurance market for asset-intensive products to mitigate risks and ensure prudence in the asset-intensive reinsurance market. It outlines the complex nature of reinsurance supervision, a global industry requiring regulators to collaborate across jurisdictions. It highlights collateral structures in the form of modified coinsurance and funds withheld as key protective features of asset-intensive reinsurance. In light of the evolving landscape and associated challenges, the BMA explains that it has implemented a tailored supervisory regime, with enhancements that include prior regulatory approval for long-term block reinsurance transactions and updated capital requirements. 

[b]What's next? [/b]The BMA views reinsurance as a proven tool for risk management and can enhance the protection of and access to insurance by increasing the capacity, diversification, and stability of the insurance sector and enabling insurers to offer more affordable and suitable products and services to customers. Possibly related to the NAIC's recent initiatives to revise actuarial standards and reporting related to asset-intensive reinsurance transactions, the BMA makes clear its support of initiatives by ceding jurisdictions to have insurers demonstrate adequate risk management to identify, measure, monitor, manage, and report the risks to which they are exposed.

results.Name: Proposed Enhancements to Public Disclosure Regime: Public Disclosure of Assets and Liabilities for Commercial Long-term Insurers
results.notes:
[color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]BMA Proposes Powerful Market Oversight Through Public Disclosure of Life & Annuity Investments[/color]

[color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]Aligned with its ongoing efforts to enhance transparency, the Bermuda Monetary Authority (BMA) posted a request for comment on [/color][color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://cdn.bma.bm/documents/2024-12-23-16-49-15-Consultation-Paper---Proposed-Enhancements-to-Public-Disclosure-Regime---Public-Disclosure-of-Assets-and-Liabilities-for-Commercial-Long-term-Insurers.pdf][i]Proposed Enhancements to Public Disclosure Regime: Public Disclosure of Assets and Liabilities for Commercial Long-term Insurers[/i][/url][/color][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]. The proposed public disclosure of assets and liabilities responds to the broader global trend and structural shifts in the life and annuity sector, which has seen an increased appetite for illiquid, hard-to-value assets that are not publicly traded and can be more complex than liquid traded assets. The [/color][color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://cdn.bma.bm/documents/2024-12-30-10-04-08-Template-for-Public-Disclosure-of-Assets-and-Liabilities.xlsm]Template for Public Disclosure of Assets and Liabilities[/url][/color][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)] would have insurers provide instrument-level (i.e., by asset CUSIP/ISIN/International ID) public disclosure of long-term assets, along with disclosure of liability profiles in a way that intends to allow for a public assessment of investments and their appropriateness. This level of disclosure aligns the BMA more closely with NAIC reporting guidelines and provides what we view as a powerful market oversight mechanism. The proposal is closely linked to the recent [/color][color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://www.bma.bm/viewPDF/documents/2024-12-11-11-27-51-Consultation-Paper---Proposed-Instructions-and-Guidanceon-the-Application-of-the-Prudent-Person-Principle.pdf][i]Proposed Instructions and Guidance on the Application of the Prudent Person Principle[/i][/url][/color][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)], which requires that an insurer only assume investment risks that it can properly identify, measure, respond to, monitor, control, and report on while considering its capital requirements and adequacy, short-term and long-term liquidity requirements, and policyholder obligations (see [/color][color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://mailchi.mp/bridgewayanalytics/newsreel-17315081?e=9ea3ec665c]ART Newsreel | December 5, 2024[/url][/color][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]). [/color]

[b][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]What's next?[/color][/b][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)] The request for comment is open through February 28, 2025. The BMA intends the new requirements to become effective on December 31, 2025, with a voluntary opt-in starting December 31, 2024.[/color]

results.Name: Assessment of Climate-Related Vulnerabilities Analytical Framework and Toolkit
results.notes:
[color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]The Financial Stability Board (FSB) published its [/color][color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://www.fsb.org/uploads/P160125.pdf][i]Assessment of Climate-Related Vulnerabilities Analytical Framework and Toolkit[/i][/url][/color][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)] on January 16, 2025. The FSB, which includes the U.S. Federal Reserve, Treasury, the Securities and Exchange Commission, and representatives from G20 economies, was formed in 2009 to coordinate national financial authorities and international standard-setting bodies to develop strong regulatory, supervisory, and other financial sector policies.[/color]

[color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]The report assesses climate-related financial vulnerabilities in the global financial system, aiming to address financial stability risks arising from interconnected physical and transition climate risks. It outlines how climate shocks (e.g., extreme weather or policy shifts) interact with existing financial vulnerabilities (e.g., asset mispricing, high leverage) to amplify risks. Key channels include credit, market, liquidity, and underwriting risks. [/color]

[color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]The toolkit is comprised of three main categories of metrics: (1) Proxies to signal potential climate risk drivers, (2) Exposure metrics to provide insights into how risks propagate through the financial system, and (3) Risk metrics to quantify financial impacts on institutions and systems.[/color]

[color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]To demonstrate the framework’s application, the report examines the potential financial stability consequences of a physical climate risk via real estate markets to demonstrate how the framework can be applied in practice. The analysis involves a severe yet arguably plausible conceptual scenario of how a physical climate shock to the real estate sector may affect financial stability if insurance becomes less available, which causes risks to shift to households, businesses, or governments.[/color]

[b][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]What's next? [/color][/b][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]The report outlines the FSB's continued development and operationalization of the framework and toolkit, focusing on refining metrics, addressing data gaps, and advancing analytical capabilities. The FSB will rely on the analytical work of member authorities and international organizations, such as the International Association of Insurance Supervisors (IAIS) and the International Organization of Securities Commissions (IOSCO), to advance work on the metrics.[/color]

results.Name: U.K. Prudential Regulatory Authority (PRA)'s January 9, 2025, Letter to Chief Executive Officers of U.K. Insurers
results.notes:
The U.K. Prudential Regulatory Authority (PRA)'s [color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://www.bankofengland.co.uk/-/media/boe/files/prudential-regulation/letter/2025/insurance-supervision-2025-priorities.pdf]January 9, 2025, Letter to Chief Executive Officers of U.K. Insurers[/url][/color] outlines the sector's 2025 thematic priorities. With the concluding year-end 2024 Solvency II review and reinstatement of assimilated law into the PRA Rulebook and other policy materials, the PRA completed all the steps necessary to implement U.K. Solvency, which initiated with Brexit. 

In 2025, the PRA priorities include spurring a vibrant, innovative, and internationally competitive insurance sector and supporting firms to make long-term investments to support growth. To this end:
[ml][ul][li indent=0 align=left]The PRA has set up a new Matching Adjustment (MA) permissions team, which allows for assessing MA applications more quickly and facilitates the broader range of investment opportunities available within the MA portfolio. The new MA guidelines allow U.K. firms more flexibility by expanding the set of assets given preferential treatment through ALM initiatives. In 2025, it will explore other steps to support insurers take advantage of the reforms, including the new MA Investment Accelerator. [/li][li indent=0 align=left]The PRA has implemented a new ‘mobilization’ regime within its New Insurers Startup Unit, designed to support potential new insurers seeking to establish themselves in the U.K.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]The PRA has streamlined authorizing and supervising third-country branches.[/li][/ul][/ml]
The PRA is also taking on several other initiatives that are tied to competitiveness and growth:
[ml][ul][li indent=0 align=left]The bulk purchase annuity (BPA) market has grown rapidly in the U.K., given the continued high demand for corporate-defined pension scheme risk transfer solutions. The structure and complexity of BPA transactions have been evolving with demand, and the PRA expects firms' risk management and control frameworks to keep up.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]Funded (i.e., asset-intensive) reinsurance is an area of focus, given that its growth could, if not adequately controlled, lead to a rapid buildup of risks in the sector and have the potential to pose systemic risks. The letter clarifies that firms’ self-assessments show they are not fully meeting supervisory expectations.[/li][/ul][/ml]
The PRA is looking to improve its ability to evaluate and maintain resilience:
[ml][ul][li indent=0 align=left]The Life Insurance Stress Test (LIST) will be launched in January 2025 and will provide insights into firm-level financial resilience for the first time for roughly a dozen insurers. The PRA also intends to publish aggregate results and provide additional context to support transparency and enhance understanding of how firms’ financial positions evolve under stress.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]Liquidity resilience is another area of focus, partly motivated by the market liquidity stresses in recent years, including the ‘Dash for Cash’ and the LDI crisis. The PRA plans to improve liquidity reporting, encouraging relevant insurers to sign up for the Bank of England’s new collateralized loan facility for non-banks and follow up on their thematic review of insurers’ liquidity risk management.[/li][/ul][/ml]

results.notes:
The U.S. Department of the Treasury Federal Insurance Office (FIO) published its January 2025 report, [color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://home.treasury.gov/system/files/311/Analyses_of_US_Homeowners_Insurance_Markets_2018-2022_Climate-Related_Risks_and_Other_Factors_0.pdf]Analyses of U.S. Homeowners Insurance Markets, 2018-2022: Climate-Related Risks and Other Factors[/url][/color]. The Report analyzes a new dataset created through a collaboration among the NAIC, state insurance regulators, and FIO, whereby the NAIC has collected data to assess climate-related financial risks in insurance. The dataset represents approximately 80% of premiums written by insurers for an average of 49 million private homeowners policies annually in the U.S., aggregated at the ZIP Code level. The Report distinguishes [i]climate-related disasters[/i], such as hurricanes and wildfires, from non-weather-related natural phenomena, such as earthquakes and earthquake-related tsunamis. From 2018 through 2022—the five-year period for the data analyzed in this Report—the annual number of major disaster declarations for climate-related events was almost double the average over the 50-year period from 1960 to 2010.

[b]A few key findings that caught our eye:[/b]
[ml][ul][li indent=0 align=left]Between 2018 and 2022, average premiums increased 8.7% faster than inflation, with an increase of 14.7% in the top 20% of ZIP Codes and a decline of at least 1.4% in the lowest 20%.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]From a regional perspective, different predominant climate-related perils resulted in diverse claims and loss patterns. Hurricanes accounted for more expected annual losses to buildings than other perils in the United States. They were associated with the highest premiums per policy and nonrenewal rates in the seven U.S. regions.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]Factors driving these trends include inflation in building costs, shifts in population to high-risk areas, rising reinsurance costs, and regulatory and litigation-related dynamics, further compounded by increasing disaster frequency and severity.[/li][/ul][/ml]
[color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]FIO plans to continue collaborating with state insurance regulators, the NAIC, and other stakeholders to monitor and address challenges in the U.S. homeowners insurance market. The FIO recommendations include enhancing data collection, proactive risk mitigation, and coordinated efforts among federal, state, and private stakeholders to address affordability and accessibility challenges in high-risk regions. The Treasury is releasing a subset of the aggregated[/color]
[color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]data metrics underlying the analysis in the Report on its [/color][color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://home.treasury.gov/policy-issues/financial-markets-financial-institutions-and-fiscal-service/federal-insurance-office/reports-notices]website[/url][/color][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)].[/color]

results.Name: Structural Shifts in the Life Insurance Sector - Consultation
results.notes:
The International Association of Insurance Supervisors (IAIS) published its March 2025 draft Issue Paper on [color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://www.iais.org/uploads/2025/03/Public-consultation-Draft-Issues-Paper-on-structural-shifts-in-the-life-insurance-sector.pdf][i]Structural Shifts in the Life Insurance Sector[/i][/url][/color] for public comment. The paper analyzes structural shifts within the life insurance sector, focusing on the increased allocation to alternative assets and the adoption of asset-intensive reinsurance (AIR) agreements. It highlights the benefits of alternative assets offering diversification, higher potential returns, and alignment with long-term liabilities. However, these benefits come with risks like valuation uncertainty, illiquidity, and complexity, requiring robust risk management. The paper includes potential areas for enhancement in the IAIS supervisory material. The Issue Paper explores:
[ml][ul][li indent=0 align=left]Investments in alternative assets.[/li][ul data=1][li indent=1 align=left]A principles-based definition of alternative assets, focusing on valuation uncertainty, illiquidity, and complexity. [/li][li indent=1 align=left]Current trends in alternative assets and their liquidity and credit risk. [/li][li indent=1 align=left]An evaluation of credit ratings and regulatory capital-related issues associated with alternative assets.[/li][/ul][li indent=0 align=left]Jurisdictional differences in reserving, capital requirements, investment flexibility, and the rise in AIR activity.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]A framework for understanding macroprudential and financial stability implications under the IAIS HF.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]Potential areas for enhancement in the IAIS supervisory material.[/li][/ul][/ml]
[b]Context[/b]. The IAIS represents insurance regulators and supervisors of more than 200 jurisdictions, constituting 97% of the world's insurance premiums. The IAIS is the principal international organization of insurance supervisors, creating international standards and guidelines on insurance supervision. The IAIS has no regulatory power or legal authority, but it influences national and regional regulators by publishing supervisory and supporting material and classifying IAIGs. ICS aspires to create a common language for supervisory discussions of group solvency and enhance global convergence among group capital standards.

[b]The details[/b]. 

[b]Investments in alternative assets. [/b]As explained in the paper, the classification and regulatory treatment of alternative assets varies widely across jurisdictions, reflecting distinct capital market characteristics and stages of development, as well as regulatory frameworks and philosophies. With that in mind, based on IAIS data, in combination with the survey's findings, the paper concludes that while alternative assets currently do not pose a global financial stability concern, there might be prudential supervision concerns for certain insurers—which is largely in line with the findings of the GIMAR 2024. It points to limited holdings by U.S. insurers under Schedule BA-[i]Other Long-Term Investments, [/i]although it acknowledges that alternative assets structured as bonds under Schedule BA have characteristics of alternative assets. Meanwhile, the paper points to European insurers’ alternative asset holdings of [b]€[/b]1,397.1 billion as of June 2024, backing traditional saving product liabilities, which increased from 8% in 2017 to about 15% in 2023.

One of the primary challenges in regulating and monitoring alternative assets is the absence of a universally accepted definition. Surveys conducted by the IAIS noted that jurisdictions employ various terms such as [i]other assets[/i], [i]non-traditional investments[/i], or [i]high-risk assets[/i] to describe investments that do not fall under more commonly used categories. This lack of definition leads to significant variations in how these assets are classified and subsequently regulated. For instance, the surveys indicated that equity funds and real estate are considered non-traditional assets in some jurisdictions, while in others, they are deemed traditional investments. 

The paper defines alternative assets as follows: 
[center][i]Alternative assets are assets which display a high degree of either valuation uncertainty, illiquidity or complexity, or a combination of these[/i].[/center]
Figure 2, which we reproduce, presents asset classes based on three supervisory rankings using a qualitative assessment.
[center][img width=500]https://mcusercontent.com/79c3cb5ae113df9ea96a5e443/images/896fec54-c5f9-6d15-1eb9-0fce34df5fec.png[/img][/center]
Figure 3, reproduced, equally weights the three supervisory rankings at a globally aggregated level. PE funds meet most of the illiquidity, complexity, and difficulty-to-value principles, followed by hedge funds, infrastructure, private credit, and unlisted property funds.
[center][img width=500]https://mcusercontent.com/79c3cb5ae113df9ea96a5e443/images/245515a4-8ad9-2b90-adf9-7b560316dfe6.png[/img][/center]
[b]The use of agency ratings and regulatory oversight.[/b] The paper highlights the significant variation in practices regarding credit ratings and regulatory capital-related issues associated with alternative assets. Credit ratings play a crucial role in evaluating the risk of alternative assets, particularly those that are complex and less transparent, and are often used by insurers to assess asset quality and determine capital requirements. In the U.S., for example, insurers use external credit ratings to determine capital charges, while other jurisdictions generally rely on internal assessments. The concern, in part, the conflicts of interest, is the potential, for example, to [i]rating shop[/i], where an insurer seeks the most favorable ratings across different agencies. This practice can lead to inflated ratings that do not accurately reflect underlying risk, potentially undermining financial stability. The paper also highlights the different capital requirements across jurisdictions, noting that the rules are changing. For example, the NAIC and other regulators are reviewing capital charges for structured securities, including CLOs, to ensure capital frameworks appropriately reflect underlying risks.

[b]Jurisdictional differences in reserving, capital requirements, and investment flexibility and the rise in AIR activity[/b]. AIR is a reinsurance risk-transfer arrangement between two entities and is characterized by a transfer of significant investment risks associated with some insurance liabilities. The paper goes through an impressive breakdown of the regulatory differences across regimes and the potential incentives each generates, relating them with the material drivers contributing to AIR's growth. It also discusses specific concerns, including recapture risk, counterparty risks, and regulatory arbitrage. Drivers of growth in using AIR are jurisdictional differences in reserving approaches, capital requirements, and investment flexibility. It is also clear that there are other important drivers:
[ml][ul][li indent=0 align=left]For third-party AIR, there may be an ability to indirectly benefit from greater investment flexibility or lower cost of capital of the reinsurer or their affiliated alternative asset manager in the form of improved reinsurance pricing.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]For third-party AIR, ceding insurers also noted the increased appetite for legacy blocks of business from AIR reinsurers. AIR allows ceding insurers to exit some markets or blocks of business effectively to refocus their attention on managing their priority products.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]For affiliated AIR, there may be a preference for risks to be transferred to a market-consistent regime, as this allows for better risk measurement and hedging and reduces what some view as redundant capital and reserve requirements.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]For affiliated AIR, access to capital and financing markets may also be a driver, as one jurisdiction may have greater access to global investors than another, even if it may come at the cost of greater total asset requirements (TAR).[/li][/ul][/ml][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]Thematically, the most significant differences are attributed to the degree to which supervisors prescribe assumptions compared to jurisdictions that allow more insurer discretion in setting assumptions, subject to supervisor oversight and limitations.[/color]


[b][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]A framework for understanding macroprudential and financial stability implications under the IAIS HF[/color][/b][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]. The key objective of macroprudential policy for the insurance sector is to ensure that the financial system and insurers can absorb rather than amplify adverse shocks. While alternative asset investments and AIR offer several benefits, they can pose financial stability risks. The [/color][color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://www.iais.org/uploads/2022/01/191114-Holistic-Framework-for-Systemic-Risk.pdf][i]IAIS Holistic Framework for Systemic Risk in Insurance (HF)[/i][/url][/color][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)] recognizes three key transmission channels for systemic risk: asset liquidation, interconnectedness (direct and indirect exposure channel), and critical functions. The insurance sector is linked to several key participants in the financial system and the real economy:[/color]
[ml][ul][li indent=0 align=left]Alternative assets can be funding vehicles for real economy borrowers such as infrastructure projects, real estate, corporates, and consumers (as a key driver under securitization);[/li][li indent=0 align=left]Life insurers have long-dated commitments to policyholders in the real economy. This could involve pensioners who depend on the continued financial condition of the insurance sector for their pensions, which is a direct part of aggregate demand in the real economy;[/li][li indent=0 align=left]Life reinsurers in AIR arrangements may provide commitments to several insurers from across the globe;[/li][li indent=0 align=left]Life insurers are also increasingly connected to a broader range of financial market participants, like banks and alternative asset managers, either as providers of finance or through the distribution of risks. Consequently, disruptions in the life insurance sector can be transmitted to the financial system and real economy.[/li][/ul][/ml][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]The paper identifies three key risks:[/color]
[ml][ol][li indent=0 align=left]Insurers’ forced liquidation of assets in stress;[/li][li indent=0 align=left]Rapid pullback of insurers from key lending markets due to defaults and downgrades;[/li][li indent=0 align=left]Financial market disruption from a mass recapture of AIR by one or many insurers.[/li][/ol][/ml]
[b][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]Potential areas for enhancement in the IAIS supervisory material[/color][/b][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]. The analysis identified gaps in supervisory approaches across different Insurance Core Principles (ICPs), highlighting areas that require updates to ensure effective risk management, including:[/color]
[ml][ul][li indent=0 align=left]ICP 3: Information Sharing and Confidentiality Requirement. Strengthen collaboration between insurance and non-insurance supervisors, particularly for monitoring cross-border AIR agreements.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]ICP 7: Corporate Governance. Require strong governance frameworks for insurers involved in complex reinsurance agreements or alternative investments​.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]ICP 8: Risk Management and Internal Control. Ensure insurers have the technical expertise to assess alternative asset risks, reinsurance complexities, and embedded leverage.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]ICP 9: Supervisory Review and Reporting. Expand supervisory review to assess liquidity risks, recapture risk, and exposures to alternative assets.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]ICP 13: Reinsurance and Other Forms of Risk Transfer. Improve transparency on affiliated reinsurance transactions and their impact on capital adequacy and liquidity.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]ICP 14: Valuation. Ensure valuation methodologies appropriately capture illiquidity, complexity, and hidden leverage in alternative assets.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]ICP 15: Investments. Improved risk management over investment concentrations that take into consideration direct risks (e.g., recapture risk) and indirect risks (e.g., underlying concentration to credit risks in the AIR collateral pools).[/li][li indent=0 align=left]ICP 16: Enterprise Risk Management (ERM). Strengthen risk identification processes for liquidity risks, hidden leverage, and asset-liability mismatches in AIR agreements.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]ICP 20: Public Disclosure. Improve disclosure requirements on alternative asset investments, valuation methodologies, and stress testing results.[/li][/ul][/ml][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)] [/color]
[b]What's next?[/b] The public comment period ends on May 19, 2025, and a background session webinar will be held on April 3, 2025, 7:00 – 8:30 AM ET (register [color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://www.iais.org/events/public-background-session-for-the-draft-issues-paper-on-structural-shifts-in-the-life-insurance-sector/]here[/url][/color]). [i]Questions for public consultation on draft Issues Paper on structural shifts in the life insurance[/i] sector can be accessed ([color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://www.iais.org/uploads/2025/03/Questions-for-public-consultation-on-draft-Issues-Paper-on-structural-shifts-in-the-life-insurance-sector.docx]here[/url][/color]). The final paper will incorporate consultation feedback and be published later in 2025.

[b]As a reminder, while the IAIS has no legal authority, its standards, guidelines, and recommendations influence national and regional regulators, shaping supervisory frameworks and insurance regulatory policies worldwide.[/b]

results.Name: Middle Market (MM) Collateralized Loan Obligations (CLOs) Primer
results.notes:
Middle Market CLOs

[b]The NAIC Capital Markets Bureau published its report, [/b][color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://content.naic.org/sites/default/files/capital-markets-mm-clo-primer.pdf][b][i]Middle Market (MM) Collateralized Loan Obligations (CLOs) Primer[/i][/b][/url][/color], which provides an overview of the MM CLO market.[b] [/b]A few noteworthy learning points:
[ml][ul][li indent=0 align=left]MM CLOs are a subsector of the broader CLO market, where the underlying collateral is predominantly senior secured loans to small and medium-sized companies or those with less than $100 million in earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]Traditionally, MM CLOs are structured as balance sheet transactions, where loans directly made to companies by the asset managers are securitized and moved off the balance sheet as a financing trade to free up the ability to generate new loans. Asset managers and their affiliates often retain the equity tranche of the MM CLOs they originate, aligning their interests with those of the transaction’s remaining investors. Unlike broadly syndicated loans (BSL) CLOs, the capital structure of MM CLOs may be tailored to meet the needs of a specific investor group and have more diverse structures than BSL CLOs.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]MM loans have no secondary market, limited data, and pricing availability compared to BSLs, which have a secondary market; they also tend to have tighter covenants than BSLs.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]Because of the credit risk of the underlying collateral, MM CLOs are structured with additional credit enhancement to protect the investors' interests. They offer a significant spread for comparable credit risk. MM CLOs are becoming a significant proportion of the overall CLO market, especially as the private credit market has expanded.[/li][/ul][/ml][center][img width=500]https://mcusercontent.com/79c3cb5ae113df9ea96a5e443/images/9db3f4b8-ed8f-31ab-c5af-981884fef7e2.png[/img][/center]

results.Name: Functioning of the E.U. Securitization Regulations
results.notes:
E.U. Regulators Assess Securitization Regulations

The Joint Committee (JC) of the European Supervisory Authorities (ESAs) published its March 31, 2025, report evaluating the [color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://www.eiopa.europa.eu/document/download/841030fd-55a0-4d46-801c-44b540a0876f_en?filename=Joint%20Committee%20report%20on%20the%20functioning%20of%20the%20securitisation%20regulation%20-%20March%202025.pdf][i]Functioning of the E.U. Securitisation Regulations[/i][/url][/color]. The report has two main objectives:
[ml][ol][li indent=0 align=left]Assess the extent to which the securitization regulatory framework (i.e., Regulation (E.U.) 2017/2402, SECR) has achieved its original policy objectives since its implementation in 2019.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]Provide legislative recommendations to the European Commission, including simplifying and strengthening the E.U. securitization framework and safeguarding financial stability.[/li][/ol][/ml]
[b][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]Context[/color][/b][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]. The European Supervisory Authorities (ESAs), the trifectorate of E.U. rulemaking bodies, are comprised of the European Banking Authority (EBA), the European Insurance and Occupational Pension Authority (EIOPA), and European Securities and Markets Authority (ESMA). They are responsible for preparing technical standards for the European Commission based on E.U. Regulations and Directives.[/color]

[color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]Acknowledging that securitization can provide greater financing opportunities, on October 9, 2024, the European Commission requested comment on the [/color][color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://finance.ec.europa.eu/document/download/fb451cdc-4e5b-4d74-9411-cb8bd0789090_en?filename=2024-eu-securitisation-framework-consultation-document_en.pdf][i]Functioning of the Securitization Framework[/i][/url][/color][i][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]. [/color][/i][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]Subsequently, it provided the [/color][color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://finance.ec.europa.eu/document/download/35846dc8-8ebc-4bc9-967d-de77ef5d9234_en?filename=2024-eu-securitisation-framework-summary-of-responses_en.pdf][i]Summary Report of the Targeted Consultation on the Functioning of the E.U. Securitization Framework[/i][/url][/color][i][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]. [/color][/i][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]The JC report focuses on recommendations to enhance the functioning of the SECR, with recommendations that are aligned with those of the Financial Stability Board’s (FSB) report on the [/color][color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://www.fsb.org/uploads/P220125-1.pdf][i]Effects of the G20 Financial Regulatory Reforms on Securitisation[/i][/url][/color][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)] (see [/color][color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://mailchi.mp/bridgewayanalytics/newsreel-17316436?e=9ea3ec665c]ART Newsreel | January 23, 2024[/url][/color][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]). The conclusions of the FSB report are contentious, with many [/color][color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://www.fsb.org/uploads/P220125-2.pdf]respondents arguing[/url][/color][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)] that the reforms impeded E.U. securitization market development compared to the U.S., which did not implement comparable reforms. Stakeholder responses to the ESA report repeatedly point to overly conservative capital charges, regulatory complexity, and a lack of investor confidence—especially outside STS deals.[/color]

[b][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]What's new? [/color][/b][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]The report identifies areas where the regulatory and supervisory framework can be enhanced, supporting the growth of robust and sound securitization markets in Europe:[/color]
[b][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]1. Clarifying the jurisdictional scope.[/color][/b][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)] Clarify that SECR should only apply if at least one securitization party (sell-side or buy-side) is established within the E.U.[/color]
[b][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]2. Broadening the definition of public securitization. [/color][/b][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]The recommended scope would include issues (1) with a prospectus approved under the E.U. Prospectus Regulation, (2) admitted to trading on E.U.-regulated markets or multilateral trading facilities (MTFs), or[/color]
[color=rgb(34, 34, 34)](3) marketed broadly with non-negotiable terms and subject to a market test requiring E.U. originators or sponsors to demonstrate that transactions are not offered to an undefined public.[/color]
[b][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]3. Introducing proportionality in due diligence requirements.[/color][/b][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)] The report calls for more proportionate and practical due diligence requirements, enabling institutional investors to receive data in formats that support meaningful risk assessment. It also calls for commitments from sell-side parties to provide ongoing information throughout the transaction's life.[/color]
[b][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]4. Targeted changes to the STS framework. [/color][/b][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]Under Solvency II, the treatment of securitizations is primarily determined by two characteristics: (1) whether a tranche is the senior-most and (2) whether the structure is classified as simple, transparent, and standardized (STS). Tranches lacking either of these characteristics are subject to significantly higher capital charges. Actively managed structures like Collateralised Loan Obligations (CLOs) or Commercial Mortgage-Backed Securities (CMBS) with mismatched maturity profiles typically fail to meet STS criteria and are thus penalized. The report proposes focused adjustments to improve the efficiency of the STS framework, particularly on-balance-sheet securitizations introduced under the Capital Markets Recovery Package, which aims to support real-economy lending. For context, the European banks rely much more heavily on on-balance-sheet securitizations.[/color]
[b][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]5. Clarifying risk retention rules.[/color][/b][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)] Clearer guidance on risk retention is recommended to reduce interpretation challenges, particularly for Collateralised Loan Obligations (CLOs) and including the term [/color][i][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]predominant source of revenues[/color][/i][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)].[/color]
[b][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]6. Simplifying transparency and reporting requirements.[/color][/b][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)] Recommendations include streamlining reporting templates for public securitizations, improving data standardization, and introducing flexibility to use aggregated or stratified data for certain asset classes. The report also suggests targeted exemptions for small and medium-sized reporting entities to reduce compliance burdens.[/color]
[b][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]7. Promoting greater supervisory consistency across the E.U. [/color][/b][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]The need for more substantial supervisory convergence is highlighted to prevent fragmentation and ensure consistent application across the E.U.[/color]

[b][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]The details.[/color][/b]The report highlights the growth in the E.U. securitization market, especially following the introduction of the STS framework for synthetic securitizations in 2021. By 2022, the share of synthetic securitizations reached 53%, up from 32% in 2020, underscoring the increasing use of synthetic securitizations as a tool for capital management and risk transfer (Figure 1 reproduced). Of course, the market is a tiny fraction of the U.S. market (see ESMA report on [color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://www.esma.europa.eu/sites/default/files/2023-09/ESMA50-524821-2908_TRV_risk_analysis_-_EU_securitisation_markets_overview.pdf]E.U. Securitization Market[/url][/color]) and is still underperforming relative to its pre-crisis peak.
[center][img width=500]https://mcusercontent.com/79c3cb5ae113df9ea96a5e443/images/35cb1a57-5084-9401-2a90-2de4b3d9f90a.png[/img][/center]

The report points to the growth in STS transactions, which comprised 21% of transactions in 2020 and reached 55% of the total E.U. securitization issuance volumes in 2023 (Figure 4 reproduced).
 
[center][img width=500]https://mcusercontent.com/79c3cb5ae113df9ea96a5e443/images/8d5c7a27-0959-3b86-bb75-9a53972245bd.png[/img][/center]

[b]What's next?[/b] The European Commission is expected to use the Joint Committee’s findings and the results of the targeted consultation to inform potential legislative amendments to SECR. These may be proposed later in 2025 as part of the broader Capital Markets Union (CMU) initiative. A synopsis report will accompany the Commission’s impact assessment, incorporating market feedback, with further consultations and feasibility studies likely to follow before any formal legislative proposals are introduced.

results.Name: International Portfolio Frictions
results.notes:
The European Insurance and Occupational Pensions Authority (EIOPA) published its report on [color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://www.eiopa.europa.eu/document/download/5eb5d143-c355-49b2-9427-75ded912dc3c_en?filename=EIOPA%20Occasional%20Research%20Paper%20-%20International%20Portfolios%20Frictions%20-%20April%202025.pdf][i]International Portfolio Frictions[/i][/url][/color][i] [/i]in collaboration with external researchers. The research reveals international portfolio frictions – barriers or challenges European investors face when attempting to diversify their portfolios across borders – that traditional frictions like home country or home currency biases cannot explain. Several patterns may be worth looking into for those of you active with cross-border insurers.  
[ml][ul][li indent=0 align=left]Despite having access to global capital markets and being regulated under common pan-European regulations, insurers' portfolio allocations show large cross-country heterogeneity/ Government bonds comprise a wide range across countries, from less than 20% for insurers domiciled in Norway to ~90% for insurers domiciled in Hungary. The standard deviation across countries of the portfolio share in corporate bonds equals 19% across countries.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]The composition of domestic fixed-income markets, as measured by the share of corporate bonds in outstanding domestic fixed-income instruments, is strongly positively correlated with the fraction of the overall portfolio invested in corporate bonds. This is surprising since European insurers do not exhibit a strong bias toward their home country. In fact, the share of total investments invested domestically averages less than 50%. The relationship also holds within the euro area, implying that home currency bias cannot explain the fact.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]In countries where insurers have higher corporate bond shares in their domestic fixed-income portfolios, their foreign investments tend to resemble their domestic portfolio and feature more corporate bonds. A standard portfolio model predicts that while domestic investments track domestic characteristics due to home bias, foreign investments should offset the characteristics of domestic financial markets.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]While the holdings of foreign bonds resemble the domestic portfolio, one might expect that when a multinational insurer operates a subsidiary in another jurisdiction, they use this as an opportunity to form a globally optimal portfolio. In contrast to this hypothesis, the facts point to the opposite, with local subsidiaries of multinational groups having similar portfolio allocations as other local insurers in their market. The fact that large multinational companies do not use their holding of foreign bonds or use their overseas subsidiaries to form globally optimal portfolios suggests that informational frictions and foreign investment expertise are unlikely to be primary drivers of these biases.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]Asset allocation decisions vary between countries with different domestic government bond yields and sovereign credit risk levels. An insurer operating in a country with high domestic government yields, which tends to be a country with higher sovereign credit risk or inflation risk, tends to hold a portfolio of foreign bonds that earns a higher yield (and is likely to be riskier). This is another example of “domestic projection bias.”[/li][/ul][/ml][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]The study concludes that the characteristics of domestic financial markets dictate European insurers’ overall portfolio allocation. This allocation pattern occurs despite a significant fraction of their assets being invested abroad and despite active local market participation by large multinational insurance groups.[/color]

results.Name: Correlation in Life Risk Based Capital - Recommendations from the Academy (Attachment 12)
results.notes:
The Academy is proposing to refine the treatment of covariance across life RBC 'C-risks' (e.g., bond investment under C-1o and insurance policies under C-2) and possibly covariance within individual C-risks (e.g., real estate, mortgages, and bonds). Under the current formula, risks are generally assumed to be completely independent or perfectly correlated, and the impact of these assumptions on RBC can be significant. Unaffiliated common stock, for example, is treated as uncorrelated with other investments and often associated with receiving significant RBC covariance relief.
[b]What's new?[/b] The presentation includes recommendations that can have significant implications for investment strategy, which is the narrow focus of our coverage, and includes changing:
[ml][ul][li indent=0 align=left]Credit-Equity correlation factor from 0% to 50%;[/li][li indent=0 align=left]Credit-Interest Rate correlation factor from 100% to 25%;[/li][li indent=0 align=left]Equity-Interest Rate correlation factor from 0% to 50%.[/li][/ul][/ml]The presentation analyzes an impact analysis on a hypothetical company with a risk distribution equal to the 2023 aggregate industry. The Academy's recommendation would increase the after-covariance required capital for Equity and Credit.
[ml][ul][li indent=0 align=left]Equity. For the hypothetical portfolio, equity RBC would increase by ~48%, with companies more heavily invested in equity impacted more. The ~48% represents the ratio of the current covariance-adjusted RBC of 56%, and the resulting Academy-recommended covariance-adjusted RBC of 83%.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]Credit. For the hypothetical portfolio, credit RBC would increase by ~6.5%. The ~6.5% represents the ratio of the current covariance-adjusted RBC of 77% to the resulting Academy-recommended covariance-adjusted RBC of 82%.[/li][/ul][/ml]The recommended framework's net impact on investment strategy makes equity investments much less attractive. The total change in RBC under the recommended approach is a mild 1.6%, with the higher RBC for Credit and Equity offset by Business, Insurance, and Interest Rates. 
[center][img width=500]https://mcusercontent.com/79c3cb5ae113df9ea96a5e443/images/0a9ec67c-f8a4-3d9d-a43a-1b94e00b4997.png[/img][/center]
[b]What's next?[/b] NAIC staff will prepare the material for public comment. Given the potential significant implications for investment and business strategies, with equity and to some extent, credit, allocated higher RBC, we expect the community to engage in the process actively.

results.Name: Leverage in Non-Bank Financial Intermediation (NBFI)
results.notes:
[color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]The Financial Stability Board (FSB) published [/color][color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://www.fsb.org/2025/03/public-responses-to-consultation-on-leverage-in-non-bank-financial-intermediation/][i]Public Responses[/i][/url][/color][i][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)] [/color][/i][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]to its December 18, 2024, consultation (i.e., request for comment), [/color][color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://www.fsb.org/uploads/P181224.pdf][i]Leverage in Non-Bank Financial Intermediation (NBFI)[/i][/url][/color][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]. The report highlights systemic vulnerabilities, particularly through derivatives and securities financing. It focuses on leverage practices among NBFIs, including insurers, and proposes improved data collection, enhanced disclosures, and harmonized metrics. Recommended policy tools include activity-based measures (e.g., minimum haircuts, enhanced margining) and entity-based tools (e.g., direct/indirect leverage caps).[/color]
[color=rgb(34, 34, 34)] [/color]
[b][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]Context[/color][/b][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]. The FSB, which includes the U.S. Federal Reserve, Treasury, and Securities and Exchange Commission, along with representatives from G20 economies, was formed in 2009 to coordinate national financial authorities and international standard-setting bodies to develop strong regulatory, supervisory, and other financial sector policies. While the FSB lacks binding authority, its views are highly influential in shaping regulatory agendas.[/color]

[b][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]What's new?[/color][/b][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)] ~ Thirty posted comment letters, seeking to help shape the final report, underscore the FSB’s potential influence on global regulatory practices. While perspectives varied across jurisdictions, responses from the U.S. insurance investment community strongly cautioned against imposing uniform standards across diverse NBFI entities. The ACLI emphasized that U.S. insurers, although technically classified as NBFIs, operate under a rigorous state-based regulatory framework with conservative leverage and robust risk management practices. Grouping them with less-regulated entities is misleading. Several other commenters echoed this view, warning that a one-size-fits-all regulatory approach fails to reflect the diverse structures, leverage practices, and safeguards within the NBFI sector.[/color]

results.Name: Analysis of Systemic Risk in the Bermuda Long-Term Insurance Market
results.notes:
Oliver Wyman published an [url=https://www.biltir.bm/images/Bermuda-systemic-risk-report-0206-vFinal.pdf][i]Analysis of Systemic Risk in the Bermuda Long-Term Insurance Market[/i][/url], sponsored by the Bermuda International Long-Term Insurers and Reinsurers (BILTIR). The report points to the estimated global retirement savings gap of ~$70 trillion and the important role that Bermuda reinsurers play in providing capital to support more than $1 trillion in life insurance reserves. It argues that Bermuda is a preferred jurisdiction in part due to its status as a global insurance hub and globally recognised regulatory regime. While the expansion of the Bermuda reinsurance sector, including asset-intensive reinsurance (AIR), has provided an important source of capital and capacity to global life insurance markets, it has also drawn attention from global regulators and observers who have noted two significant global trends in the life sector:
[ml][ul][li indent=0 align=left]Increased allocations to private credit assets on life insurer balance sheets.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]Increased use of asset-intensive reinsurance (AIR), particularly in cross-border transactions to jurisdictions such as Bermuda.[/li][/ul][/ml]These trends have raised questions as to whether the concentration of risk held by Bermuda reinsurers presents a potential [i]systemic risk,[/i] through potential transmission channels:
[ml][ul][li indent=0 align=left][i]Asset liquidation[/i], whereby institutions, including insurance companies, might be forced to sell assets en masse rapidly[/li][li indent=0 align=left][i]Interconnectedness[/i] or [i]exposure[/i], whereby important direct and indirect linkages across and between financial institutions (e.g., banks, reinsurers, investment funds), can result in the failure of one impacting another.[/li][li indent=0 align=left][i]Critical function[/i] or [i]substitutability, [/i]whereby a certain role essential to the financial system or real economy, cannot be replaced in the event that it stops playing that role en masse.[/li][li indent=0 align=left][i]Contagion,[/i] where a system-wide loss of trust could trigger panic and customer withdrawal, threatening the system as a whole.[/li][/ul][/ml]To evaluate the potential for systemic risk arising from the Bermuda long-term insurance sector, the Oliver Wyman report evaluates how the transmission channels could apply to the sector under three hypothetical scenarios:
[ml][ul][li indent=0 align=left][b]A credit crisis, which triggers mass reinsurance recapture[/b]. The report finds that AIR deals often include structural protections (collateral, investment guidelines, etc.), with Bermuda reinsurers showing stable capitalization and diversified portfolios.[/li][li indent=0 align=left][b]Confidence shock to the Bermudian insurance market, triggering mass lapse and sale of assets[/b]. The report concludes that Bermudian long-term insurers hold sufficient liquid assets to meet unexpected liquidity demands even in a severe liquidity stress, and even if material sales were required to address liquidity needs, these would likely be limited[/li][li indent=0 align=left]to liquid assets.[/li][li indent=0 align=left][b]Withdrawal of insurer's private credit demand[/b]. The report concludes that if an event caused the Bermuda long-term insurance sector to pull back from funding the private credit market, the sector constitutes a small share of global credit markets, and a pullback is unlikely to disrupt the broader financial system.[/li][/ul][/ml]
While the report concludes that the Bermuda long-term sector does not meaningfully contribute to global systemic risk, it does identify several recommendations to enhance the evaluation of potential risks:
[ml][ul][li indent=0 align=left]More public transparency on AIR structures, transactions, counterparties, and volumes as part of regular reporting would improve understanding, particularly of potential risk concentrations in the system.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]Ongoing regulatory oversight and safeguards rather than restrictions, advocating for enhanced monitoring and safeguards.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]Risk-based understanding and monitoring of asset and liability portfolios, particularly the potential risks of complex assets in the context of liability profiles.[/li][/ul][/ml]
In addition, the report relates these themes to general prudent firm-level risk management practices:
[ml][ul][li indent=0 align=left]A well-defined counterparty risk framework, informed by recapture impacts.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]Enhanced counterparty default or recapture planning.[/li][/ul][/ml]
The report positions Bermuda as a robust reinsurance hub while acknowledging that emerging trends such as AIR and private credit warrant enhanced oversight. It advocates for strengthened transparency and risk-based supervision rather than imposing blanket restrictions that might impair market function.

results.Name: Growing Pains: Clarity and Culture Change Required
results.notes:
The House of Lords, the U.K.'s upper legislative chamber, agrees, publishing its Financial Services Regulation Committee June 13, 2025, report, [url=https://publications.parliament.uk/pa/ld5901/ldselect/ldfsrc/133/133.pdf][i]Growing Pains: Clarity and Culture Change Required[/i][/url][i],[/i] pulls no punches, warning that the PRA’s [i]deeply entrenched culture of risk aversion[/i] is undermining both industry confidence and the U.K.’s broader growth strategy. It cites excessive compliance costs, overlapping remits, and slow product approvals, highlighting, for example, the U.K.’s declining share of the insurance-linked securities market due to authorization delays.

The report recommends a complete rethink, arguing authorizations must be faster, compliance burdens quantified and reduced, and supervisory intensity matched to firm complexity. It also calls for cleaning up the consumer-facing regulation spread across the Financial Conduct Authority and Financial Ombudsman Service, which currently creates regulatory ambiguity regarding what the report terms a [i]regulatory penalty[/i] on writing insurance in the U.K..

The report argues that despite the PRA's rhetoric around unlocking productive investment, its current approach to capital requirements has the opposite effect. It points to delays and rigidity in expanding the MA portfolio as a key obstacle to life insurers putting capital to work in long-term assets like infrastructure and private credit. Worse still, U.K. insurers face higher capital charges on securitized assets than they would on economically similar corporate bonds, undermining the stated goal of aligning regulation with real-world risk. As we have noted before, the legacy of Solvency II has been to hobble the market for some long-term securities and strongly discourage securitisation. In that important dimension, we agree with the Lords that it is disappointing that the PRA has been unable to capitalize on its newfound freedom to redesign Solvency to best support the U.K. market. Unless the PRA embraces meaningful cultural reform, the U.K. will continue on the path of unattractive insurance policy writing.

In response, the Bank of England (BoE) Executive Director (ED) of Prudential Policy, June 18, 2025, speech [url=https://www.bankofengland.co.uk/speech/2025/june/david-bailey-speech-at-risk-net-live-europe][i]Innovation and Regulation - Striking the Balance[/i][/url], struck a conciliatory tone, pushing back on the narrative. The mandate to improve competitiveness was emphasized, but warnings of unbridled innovation were flagged. While this could set up a confrontation between the BoE and Lawmakers, ED was keen to point out where the bank was embracing risk and pushing the envelope. 

[b]What's next?[/b] The PRA is required to respond formally within 12 months. For firms, this is the window to push for a regulatory framework fit for growth. Time will tell how this evolves!

results.Name: Another Year of Material Growth in U.S. Insurers’ Exposure to Residential Mortgage-Backed Securities (RMBS)
results.notes:
The NAIC Capital Markets Bureau published its latest report, [url=https://content.naic.org/sites/default/files/capital-markets-special-reports-rmbs-ye2024.pdf][i]Another Year of Material Growth in U.S. Insurers’ Exposure to Residential Mortgage-Backed Securities (RMBS)[/i][/url], which notes the 17% growth in 2024 holdings, the second consecutive year of double-digit growth. Last discussed in [url=https://mailchi.mp/bridgewayanalytics/newsreel-17319807?e=9ea3ec665c]ART Newsreel | May 8, 2025[/url], and [url=https://bridgewayanalytics.us8.list-manage.com/track/click?u=79c3cb5ae113df9ea96a5e443&id=df69bc3d6b&e=9ea3ec665c][i]U.S. Insurance Industry’s Cash and Invested Assets Rise Over 5% to Close in on $9 Trillion as of Year-End 2024[/i][/url], among bond holdings, the fastest-growing segments in 2024 included private-label RMBS (23%) and agency-backed RMBS (14%). A few highlights from the report: 
[ml][ul][li indent=0 align=left]Agency RMBS accounted for 68% of U.S. insurers’ RMBS exposure, and private label RMBS comprised 32%.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]Property & casualty companies continue to increase their allocation to agency RMBS. As of year-end 2024, they are the largest holders among U.S. insurers, with $150 billion, representing 49% of the industry’s total exposure.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]Life companies hold the majority share of private-label RMBS, accounting for 73% of total exposure as of year-end 2024.[/li][/ul][/ml][center][img width=500]https://mcusercontent.com/79c3cb5ae113df9ea96a5e443/images/a37a60d5-5025-78ec-0506-831aa2a38160.png[/img][/center]

[b]Favorable treatment and demand for RMBS[/b]. Last discussed in [url=https://mailchi.mp/bridgewayanalytics/newsreel-17319404?e=9ea3ec665c]ART Newsreel | April 24, 2025[/url], private label RMBS and commercial mortgage-backed securities (RMBS) receive NAIC Intrinsic Price Designations, with agency ratings not allowable. Agency MBS treatment aligns with U.S. government debt and receives 1.A Designations. Discussed extensively in the Bank of America (BoA) Global Research report, [url=https://rsch.baml.com/report?q=0J1uLSGajShCYNeD00HeWw__&e=amnon.levy%40bridgewayanalytics.com&h=TDUhiQ][i]NAIC 2024 Year-End Modeling Results for RMBS: Favorable[/i][/url], and [url=https://mailchi.mp/bridgewayanalytics/newsreel-17315959?e=9ea3ec665c#BoA]ART Newsreel | January 9, 2025[/url], private label RMBS Intrinsic Price Designations are generally significantly more favorable than agency ratings. For example, Exhibit 6 from the Bank of America report points out that Intrinsic Price Designations are more favorable than agency ratings for ~90% BBB-rated RMBS, in many cases by over six notches, with only 4% receiving less favorable Designations. All else equal, including the presumed level-set agency rating risk-ranking across asset classes, favorable Intrinsic Price Designations result in private label RMBS being very attractive from an RBC perspective. We should expect the growth in RMBS holdings to continue, given their favorable treatment, and assuming the private label RMBS supply continues to grow, as projected in the Capital Market Bureau report.

[center][img width=500]https://mcusercontent.com/79c3cb5ae113df9ea96a5e443/images/6437fd63-2f69-9838-cbb5-4185a6dae43a.png[/img][/center]

results.Name: EIOPA Financial Stability Report - June 19, 2025
results.notes:
The European Insurance and Occupational Pension Authority (EIOPA) published its June 19, 2025, [url=https://www.eiopa.europa.eu/document/download/047539e4-db06-4d56-b1f2-b3d1d3365204_en?filename=EIOPA%20Financial%20Stability%20Report%20June%202025.pdf][i]Financial Stability Report[/i][/url][i]. [/i]The report presents a detailed assessment of risks and developments across the European insurance, reinsurance, and occupational pension sectors. It points to the fragile E.U. economy, characterised by uncertainty, experiencing highly volatile equity and foreign exchange markets. It highlights ongoing insurers' investment trends and risks, pointing to challenging real estate markets and trends in alternative markets.

[b]Context[/b]. EIOPA is the European Union’s financial supervisory agency, coordinating regulation across the insurance and pensions sectors. While it does not have direct supervisory authority over most market participants, it advises the European Commission, European Parliament, and Council of the E.U., and helps shape E.U.-level policy and legislation. EIOPA is one of the three European Supervisory Authorities (ESAs) established after the global financial crisis to improve consistency and oversight in the E.U. financial system. While national regulators remain the primary supervisors of individual firms, EIOPA develops regulatory frameworks, conducts stress testing, and issues guidance and technical standards intended to drive convergence across E.U. member states. In practice, EIOPA plays a key role in policy design and in advising on reforms, rather than in direct enforcement.

[b]Highlights on investment trends, portfolio allocations, and home bias. [/b]As of end-2024, fixed-income securities remain the dominant asset class (65%) in insurer portfolios—28.7% in corporate bonds (mostly investment grade), 27.9% in government bonds, and 6.4% in mortgages. Equities comprise 21.7%, while cash, deposits, and real estate comprise the rest. A notable trend is the increased investment in alternative assets, now 17.5% of portfolios, which includes real estate (6.9%), largely through funds, mortgages, and REITs, structured notes (2.2%), and private equity (2.4%). The report references structured notes as a component of alternative investments but does not elaborate on securitization markets beyond their classification under illiquid or complex alternative investments. However, there is continued regulatory focus on capital treatment for complex and illiquid instruments, especially those related to sustainability risks. Notably, insurers display significant home bias in sovereign bond holdings. However, corporate bond and equity portfolios are more globally diversified. U.S. assets hold substantial weight: 24.9% of equities, 13.4% of corporate bonds, and 4.4% of government bonds. This geographical spread has amplified currency risk, as illustrated by recent movements in foreign exchange rates.

[b]Conclusions[/b]. The Financial Stability Report provides a cautiously optimistic outlook for the insurance and pensions sectors. The report finds no single factor dominates risk today, but the ongoing interplay of macro shocks, investment complexity, and the regulatory landscape is having insurers shift portfolio strategies:
[ml][ul][li indent=0 align=left]The move toward alternatives and structured products is enduring and likely to continue under yield-seeking pressures.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]Geopolitical and currency risks are reshaping liquidity risk management, particularly for globally invested portfolios.[/li][/ul][/ml]
